overview.whyItMatters: Choosing the right foundation model directly impacts cost, latency, and output quality. The exam heavily tests your ability to match model capabilities to requirements—this isn't about memorizing model names but understanding trade-offs between speed, cost, and capability. A single poorly-chosen model can result in 10x higher costs or unacceptable latency for your users.

concepts.title: Understanding Model Capabilities
concepts.content:
Foundation models differ dramatically in their capabilities, and understanding these differences is essential for making the right choice. The most important factors to consider are context window size, task specialization, reasoning ability, and output quality.

Context window refers to how much text the model can process in a single request. Models like Claude 3 offer 200K token windows, allowing them to analyze entire codebases or lengthy documents. Smaller models may only support 4K-8K tokens. If your use case involves long documents, you need a model with an appropriate context window—otherwise you'll need to implement chunking strategies.

Task specialization matters because models are trained with different objectives. Some excel at code generation (like CodeLlama), others at conversational interactions, and others at analytical reasoning. Claude models are known for nuanced reasoning and instruction-following. Llama models offer a balance of capability and efficiency. Amazon Titan models integrate deeply with other AWS services.

Larger models like Claude 3 Opus or Claude Sonnet excel at complex multi-step reasoning, nuanced analysis, and tasks requiring deep understanding. However, they cost more per token and have higher latency. Smaller models like Claude Haiku or Llama 3 8B are significantly faster and cheaper—often 10-20x less expensive—making them ideal for simpler tasks like classification, entity extraction, or straightforward summarization. The key insight is matching model capability to task complexity: using Opus for simple classification is wasteful, while using Haiku for complex legal analysis will produce poor results.
keyPoints: Context window: Determines maximum input size (4K to 200K+ tokens)
Latency: Larger models = slower responses (seconds vs milliseconds)
Cost: Charged per 1K input/output tokens; large models cost 10-20x more
Task fit: Match model strength to your specific use case
concepts.examTip: Questions about 'cost-effective' solutions often point to smaller/faster models for simple tasks. If a question mentions 'complex reasoning' or 'nuanced analysis,' that's a signal for larger models.

concepts.title: Evaluating and Comparing Models
concepts.content:
Selecting a model based on intuition or marketing claims is a common mistake. Instead, you should evaluate models systematically using your actual use case data. This data-driven approach ensures you choose the model that performs best for your specific requirements, not just the one that performs best on generic benchmarks.

Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluations provides a structured way to compare models. You prepare a test dataset representative of your production workload—this might include example prompts, expected outputs, and edge cases you've encountered. Then you define evaluation criteria: accuracy (how often the model produces correct answers), relevance (how well responses address the question), toxicity (presence of harmful content), or custom metrics specific to your domain.

The evaluation runs your test dataset through multiple models and produces quantitative scores. This lets you compare not just raw quality but cost-per-quality—a model that's 5% less accurate but 80% cheaper might be the better choice for your use case. You should also consider latency requirements: a model that produces perfect responses in 5 seconds may be unusable for a real-time chatbot but perfect for batch processing.

Re-evaluation is important when new models release or your requirements change. The foundation model landscape evolves rapidly, and a model that was best-in-class six months ago may now be outperformed by newer alternatives. Build evaluation into your workflow so you can continuously optimize model selection.
keyPoints: Use test datasets representative of your actual production workload
Define clear evaluation metrics before testing (accuracy, relevance, latency)
Compare cost-per-quality, not just raw quality scores
Re-evaluate periodically as new models release
concepts.examTip: Bedrock Model Evaluations is the answer when questions ask about 'objectively comparing,' 'benchmarking,' or 'systematically evaluating' models. It's the managed service for model comparison.

concepts.title: Dynamic Model Selection Patterns
concepts.content:
Production AI systems rarely use a single model for all requests. Instead, sophisticated architectures implement dynamic model selection—choosing the appropriate model at runtime based on request characteristics, cost constraints, or availability.

The simplest pattern is configuration-driven selection. Store model IDs in AWS AppConfig or Parameter Store rather than hardcoding them. Your application reads the configuration at runtime and routes requests accordingly. This enables instant model switching without code deployment—critical for A/B testing new models, responding to cost changes, or handling model deprecations.

Cascading is a powerful cost-optimization pattern. Route requests first to a fast, inexpensive model like Claude Haiku. If the model's confidence score is below a threshold, or if the response quality check fails, escalate to a more capable model like Claude Sonnet. This hybrid approach can reduce costs by 60-80% while maintaining quality for complex requests. The key is implementing reliable confidence scoring—this might use the model's own uncertainty signals or a separate classifier.

A/B testing compares models in production with real user traffic. Route a percentage of requests to a new model and measure user satisfaction, task completion rates, or other business metrics. This provides real-world performance data that benchmarks can't capture. AWS AppConfig supports percentage-based routing for this purpose.

Fallback patterns handle model unavailability. If your primary model returns errors or exceeds latency thresholds, automatically route to a backup model. This might be a different model in Bedrock, a different region via Cross-Region Inference, or even a cached/simplified response for graceful degradation.
keyPoints: AppConfig: Change models via configuration without code deployment
Cascading: Start cheap, escalate to capable models only when needed
A/B testing: Compare models with real production traffic
Fallback: Automatic switching when primary model fails or is slow

concepts.title: Model Customization Lifecycle
concepts.content:
When base foundation models don't meet your requirements despite optimized prompting, customization is the next step. However, customization adds complexity, cost, and maintenance burden—it should be a deliberate choice, not a default approach.

Fine-tuning adjusts a model's behavior using examples of desired inputs and outputs. If you need a model to always respond in a specific JSON format, provide 100+ examples of correctly formatted responses. If you want a particular tone or style, show the model many examples of that style. Fine-tuning is effective for format consistency, domain-specific terminology, and behavior adjustment. In Bedrock, you prepare a JSONL training file, configure the fine-tuning job, and receive a custom model version.

Continued pre-training (also called domain-adaptive pre-training) adds new knowledge to a model. If your organization has proprietary terminology, internal processes, or domain-specific information not in the model's training data, continued pre-training can incorporate this knowledge. This requires more data than fine-tuning—typically thousands of documents—and is computationally more expensive.

LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) adapters offer efficient fine-tuning with lower compute costs. Instead of updating all model weights, LoRA trains small adapter layers that modify the model's behavior. These adapters are smaller, faster to train, and can be swapped at inference time—allowing one base model to serve multiple customized use cases.

The Model Registry provides version control for your custom models. Track which training data produced each version, compare performance across versions, and rollback if a new version underperforms. This is essential for maintaining quality as you iterate on customization.

Critically, always exhaust prompt engineering before attempting customization. Many 'customization requirements' can be solved with better prompts, system messages, or few-shot examples—at zero training cost.
keyPoints: Fine-tuning: Adjust behavior with input/output examples (format, style, tone)
Continued pre-training: Add domain-specific knowledge to the model
LoRA adapters: Efficient fine-tuning with lower compute, swappable at inference
Model Registry: Version control for custom models with rollback capability
concepts.examTip: Fine-tuning questions mention 'specific format' or 'consistent style.' Continued pre-training mentions 'domain knowledge' or 'proprietary terminology.' Always consider prompt engineering first.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Effective model assessment requires defining evaluation criteria specific to your use case before running any tests. Generic benchmarks like MMLU or HellaSwag provide baseline comparisons but don't reflect your specific requirements.

For summarization tasks, use metrics like ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) that measure overlap between generated and reference summaries. For factual question-answering, measure accuracy against ground truth answers—be sure to account for semantically equivalent responses that differ in wording. For code generation, functional correctness (does the code execute and produce expected outputs?) matters more than syntactic similarity.

Bedrock Model Evaluations automates this workflow. You upload evaluation datasets, configure metrics, and run evaluations across multiple models simultaneously. The service produces comparison reports showing each model's performance on your criteria. This objective data prevents bias toward familiar models or vendor marketing.

Beyond accuracy metrics, consider practical factors: inference cost per request, p95 latency (the 95th percentile response time), and consistency of outputs. A model might perform excellently on average but have occasional very slow responses that break user experience.

awsServices.whenToUse: Comparing multiple models objectively using your own test datasets. Automated benchmarking with custom evaluation criteria. Generating comparison reports for model selection decisions.
Access to multiple foundation models for comparison. On-demand inference for evaluation runs. No infrastructure to manage.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Dynamic model selection decouples your application logic from specific model choices, enabling operational flexibility that hardcoded model IDs cannot provide.

The recommended pattern stores model configuration in AWS AppConfig. Create a configuration profile containing the model ID, inference parameters (temperature, max tokens), and any routing rules. Your Lambda function fetches this configuration at startup (cached for performance) and uses it to invoke the appropriate Bedrock model. When you need to switch models, update the AppConfig configuration—your application picks up the change without redeployment.

AppConfig provides additional features valuable for model management: gradual rollout (deploy new model to 10% of traffic, then 50%, then 100%), automatic rollback (revert to previous model if error rates spike), and environment-specific configuration (use cheaper models in development, premium models in production).

For simpler scenarios, AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store can hold model IDs. This provides secure storage with IAM-based access control but lacks AppConfig's gradual rollout features.

This pattern enables sophisticated operational practices: testing new models with a small traffic percentage before full rollout, instantly switching to backup models during incidents, or adjusting model selection based on time-of-day traffic patterns.

awsServices.whenToUse: Feature flags for model switching without deployment. Gradual rollouts with percentage-based traffic routing. Automatic rollback if metrics degrade.
Serverless compute that reads configuration and routes to appropriate models. Scales automatically with request volume.
Simple configuration storage for model IDs. Secure storage with IAM access control. Good for basic use cases.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Resilient AI systems must handle failures gracefully because foundation model services, like any external dependency, can experience outages, throttling, or degraded performance.

Bedrock Cross-Region Inference is the first line of defense. When enabled, Bedrock automatically routes requests to the nearest available region if your primary region experiences issues. This provides seamless failover without application changes—your code calls the same endpoint, and Bedrock handles the routing. Cross-Region Inference is particularly valuable for applications requiring high availability SLAs.

Circuit breaker patterns prevent cascade failures. When error rates exceed a threshold, stop sending requests to the failing model and immediately route to a fallback. AWS Step Functions can implement this pattern: track error counts, open the circuit when failures spike, periodically test if the service has recovered, and close the circuit to resume normal operation. This protects both your application and the downstream service from being overwhelmed.

Graceful degradation provides useful responses even when models are unavailable. Options include serving cached responses for common queries, falling back to simpler rule-based systems, or returning a message explaining reduced functionality. The key is ensuring your application remains useful rather than completely failing.

Retry logic should use exponential backoff with jitter to avoid thundering herd problems. If 1000 requests fail simultaneously and all retry at the same time, you'll likely cause another failure. Randomized delays spread out retries and improve recovery chances.

awsServices.whenToUse: Automatic regional failover without code changes. High availability for critical applications. Seamless routing to available regions.
Implement circuit breaker patterns. Orchestrate retry logic with exponential backoff. Manage complex error handling workflows.
Cache common responses for graceful degradation. Reduce load on model endpoints. Serve cached responses during outages.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Custom model lifecycle management ensures you can train, evaluate, deploy, and if necessary rollback custom models reliably. This is more complex than using base models and requires operational processes similar to traditional software deployment.

The lifecycle begins with training data preparation. Clean, high-quality training data is essential—garbage in, garbage out applies strongly to model training. Store training datasets in S3 with versioning enabled so you can track which data produced each model version.

For fine-tuning base Bedrock models, use Bedrock's built-in fine-tuning capability. Prepare a JSONL file with input/output pairs, configure the training job (learning rate, epochs), and submit. Bedrock handles the infrastructure. For more control or custom architectures, Amazon SageMaker provides full training pipeline capabilities including distributed training, hyperparameter tuning, and custom container support.

After training, evaluate the custom model against your test dataset using the same metrics you used for base model selection. Compare performance to the base model—customization should provide measurable improvement to justify the added complexity.

Model Registry tracks all model versions with metadata about training data, hyperparameters, and evaluation results. When you need to rollback, you can quickly identify and deploy a previous version.

Blue-green deployment minimizes risk when deploying new model versions. Run the new model alongside the existing one, gradually shift traffic to the new version while monitoring metrics, and quickly rollback by shifting traffic back if issues arise.

awsServices.whenToUse: Full custom model training with complete control. Complex ML pipelines requiring distributed training. Hyperparameter tuning and custom containers.
Fine-tuning base foundation models with managed infrastructure. Simpler workflow than full SageMaker training.
Store training datasets with versioning. Track data lineage for model reproducibility.

values.Large Models (Opus/Sonnet): Complex reasoning, multi-step analysis, nuanced writing, code review
values.Small Models (Haiku/Llama 8B): Classification, entity extraction, simple QA, high-volume summarization
values.Custom Fine-tuned Models: Domain-specific terminology, consistent output formats, specialized behavior
Varies by base model size and hosting configuration
Training cost + inference cost (may exceed base model)

values.Small Models (Haiku/Llama 8B): Complex reasoning, nuanced analysis, expert-level tasks
values.Custom Fine-tuned Models: When prompt engineering solves the problem, limited training data

examStrategies.approach: Start with the smallest model that can handle the task. Look for complexity indicators in the question—simple classification or extraction suggests small models, while 'complex reasoning' or 'nuanced analysis' suggests larger models. Consider cascading patterns that use cheap models first and escalate only when needed.

AWS AppConfig is the primary answer for runtime model switching. Parameter Store works for simpler cases. Look for requirements around gradual rollout or A/B testing—these point specifically to AppConfig. Eliminate any answer requiring code changes or redeployment.

Cross-Region Inference is the first answer for Bedrock availability. For broader resilience, add circuit breakers (Step Functions), caching (ElastiCache), and graceful degradation patterns. Multi-region deployment with Route 53 failover applies to the full application stack.

keyTakeaways: Match model size to task complexity—using large models for simple tasks wastes money
Bedrock Model Evaluations provides objective model comparison using your data
AWS AppConfig enables model switching without code deployment, including gradual rollouts
Cross-Region Inference provides automatic regional failover for high availability
Fine-tune for format/style consistency; continued pre-training for domain knowledge
Always exhaust prompt engineering before investing in model customization
commonMistakes: Defaulting to the largest model without evaluating whether smaller models suffice
Fine-tuning when prompt engineering with examples would achieve the same result
Deploying to a single region without Cross-Region Inference for critical applications
Hardcoding model IDs instead of using configuration for operational flexibility
Not implementing fallback patterns for graceful degradation during outages
